prompt: I want to add WebSocket support to our Go API server. Let's brainstorm the design.

assertions.text: Output does NOT contain implementation code (no .go files written, no Write/Edit tool calls for source code)
Output asks at least one clarifying question before proposing approaches
Output proposes 2-3 approaches with trade-offs for each

eval_name: Brainstorm asks questions one at a time
prompt: We need to redesign the permission system. The current one is too simple — just admin/user roles. We need something more granular.

assertions.text: First response asks ONE clarifying question, not a list of 5+
Question is asked using AskUserQuestion tool with structured options when applicable

prompt: I want to add a plugin system to arc. [After several rounds of Q&A, user approves the design.] Let's save this and move to planning.

assertions.text: Output writes the design to docs/plans/ and registers with `arc plan create`
Output presents the plan for review with approve/review options
Output suggests transitioning to the plan skill as next step
Output includes a routing analysis with work item count, parallel readiness, files touched, layers crossed, risk areas, and scale assessment before presenting transition options
The recommended option is marked in the AskUserQuestion transition prompt (e.g., '(recommended — ...)' tag on the suggested option)

prompt: Add a simple health check endpoint to the API. Just GET /healthz returning 200.

assertions.text: Output recognizes this is small/obvious work and suggests skipping brainstorm or going directly to implement
Output does NOT propose elaborate monitoring frameworks or complex health check systems for a simple endpoint

eval_name: Brainstorm identifies shared contracts for parallel readiness
prompt: Design a notification system with email, Slack, and webhook channels. Each channel will be implemented by a different agent in parallel.

assertions.text: Output identifies shared contracts (e.g., Notifier interface, NotificationPayload type) that multiple channels reference
Output presents shared contracts as a 'foundation layer' to be implemented before parallel work
Shared contracts include exact, copy-pasteable code blocks with type definitions (e.g., Go structs with field names and types), not just descriptive text like 'Notifier interface in notifier.go'
Shared contracts include contract test assertions (e.g., compile-time type checks like `var _ int64 = n.ID`) alongside the type definitions
Task-internal types are presented as typed pseudocode (e.g., `EmailConfig { smtp_host: String, port: u16 }`) separate from the exact shared contracts

eval_name: Routing analysis recommends arc:plan for multi-task work
prompt: Design a comment system for arc issues — users should be able to add, edit, and delete comments. Comments need a storage layer, API endpoints, and CLI commands. [After Q&A, user approves the design with 4 tasks across storage/API/CLI layers.]

assertions.text: Routing analysis identifies 3+ work items
Routing analysis identifies multiple layers crossed (at minimum storage and API)
Routing analysis recommends /arc:plan with a justification referencing the multi-task or multi-layer nature of the work
The 'Break into tasks with /arc:plan' option is tagged as recommended in the AskUserQuestion

eval_name: Routing analysis recommends arc:implement for single-task work
prompt: I want to add a --json flag to the `arc list` command so it outputs JSON instead of the table format. [After brief Q&A, user approves the simple design — single task, CLI layer only, no migrations.]

assertions.text: Routing analysis identifies 1 work item
Routing analysis identifies a single layer (CLI) and no risk areas
Routing analysis recommends /arc:implement with a justification referencing the small, single-task scope
The 'Implement directly with /arc:implement' option is tagged as recommended in the AskUserQuestion
